This means, to validate the current state of the embedding on the validation set in terms of link prediction, the scoring function has to be evaluated for about 2 • 260,790 • 277,002 ≈ 1.445 • 10 11 triples, since the head and the tail of each validation triple are replaced with each other entity in the snapshot. As a result, the validation together with the test of the final embedding exceeds our time limits. We decided to cancel the validation and therefore early stopping so that the online method runs for the complete number of epochs. To reduce the memory requirements, we also had to increase the number of batches for the general epochs to 200 instead of 100. Since RESCAL represents relations as matrices, we would further need to lower the batch size for this embedding model to reduce memory requirements, but then again the resulting runtime would exceed time limits. Hence, we do not provide results for RESCAL on the Higgs Twitter data set. Finally, as our stability metric requires the computation of a pairwise distance matrix between all entity embeddings, the calculation of the NMC scores exceeded memory limits, although the matrix is calculated and processed in chunks. However, as we have already evaluated the stability on NELL5 and MathOverflow, we are more interested in the scalability of the online method in terms of embedding quality and especially the runtime.

Embedding Quality All explained metrics for the measurement of the link prediction performance depend on the size of the given KG, because it is just more unlikely to calculate a small rank for a test triple in a large-scale data set than in a smaller one (especially the number of entities is responsible for this effect). Hence, to obtain comparable results, we use the Hits@100 metric instead of the previously employed MRR. The results of the translational distance models and semantic matching models (except RESCAL) are given in figs. 21 and 22.

First of all, we can observe huge performance differences between the translational distance and the semantic matching models. On average, the recalculated embeddings of DistMult and Analogy achieve about 3.8 -4.9 and 4.2 -5.4 times higher Hits@100 proportions than TransE, TransH and TransD. This could be due to the huge underlying directed network consisting of the follower relation. Furthermore, the embedding quality for the translational distance models is fluctuating from snapshot to snapshot, whereas for the semantic matching models there is a clearly visible positive trend due to the change of the dynamic KG. As before in the case of MathOverflow, the online method for DistMult and especially for Analogy is not completely able to catch up with the improving link prediction results of the recalculated embeddings. The bigger difference for Analogy could have been prevented by using validations and therefore backtracking to the best intermediate result for the initial embedding, as can be seen from the decreasing Hits@100 proportion during the later offline epochs. For DistMult the average Hits@100 score of the recalculated embeddings is just 1.47% better than for the online method. Hence, we draw the conclusion that our approach is able to maintain comparable embedding quality for large-scale dynamic KGs. Finally, we have a look at the runtime of the online method. Link Prediction without Constraints Hits@100 Filter 2_20_0.5_0 Higgs Fig. 22: Link prediction performance in the Hits@100 metric of the online method in comparison to recalculations for semantic matching models (except RESCAL) on Higgs Twitter Runtime In theory, the online method could be at most five times faster than the recalculations, as we use only a fifth of the epochs in the offline method. These epochs mainly determine the runtime as already explained in the analysis of the time complexity. For each snapshot we documented the runtime of the online method as well as of the recalculations without the time for the switch to the new snapshot or for the final link prediction test. The results in terms of average runtime per snapshot and standard deviation are given in table 5. For the faster models TransE and DistMult the online method is three times faster than the recalculations. While the other three embedding models TransH, TransD and Analogy took at most 4 hours and 38 minutes to recalculate an embedding from scratch, the online method is even four times faster with a maximum runtime of 1 hour and 8 minutes. The larger standard deviation mainly results from an up to 15 minutes slower runtime for the first snapshot because of hardware reasons, but also from variation in the changes of the dynamic KG.
Overall the online method is scalable to large real-world dynamic KGs and even saves a lot of time in comparison to recalculations.

The embedding v is then initialized as the closest point to the orthogonal lines through v 1 and v 2 .

figure.head: Fig. 4 :Fig. 5 :
figure.figDesc: Fig.4: Sketch of the general idea for the iterative approach of incorporating negative evidence in a two-dimensional embedding space

figure.head: Theorem 1
figure.figDesc: The initialization of new entities and relations (without the preinitialization) requires at most O(initT imes•|∆E + S |•(1+initN egs)) evaluations of the scoring function. Proof. In the worst case the initialization of each entity and relation never stops early. Hence, we end up with initT imes • (|∆V + | + |∆R + |) epochs for the whole initialization. In each of these epochs, the scoring function is evaluated for the triples I(r) in the case of a relation r and for the triples I(v) ∪ O(v) in the case of an entity v. However, since these are new entities and relations, we have I(r) ⊆ ∆E + S ∀r ∈ ∆R + and I(v)∪O(v) ⊆ ∆E + S ∀v ∈ ∆V + . In the worst case all new triples of the training set consist of at least one new entity or relation. Then all triples of ∆E + S are considered in the initialization. Since a triple only consists of three entries, it can only be considered for the initialization of at most one new relation and two new entities. As a result, we can omit the number of added entities and relations and give O(initT imes • |∆E + S |) as an upper approximation for the number of evaluations of the scoring function for positive triples. Finally, for each evaluation of the scoring function so far, we sample another initN egs corrupted triples and evaluate the scoring function on them. This results in O(initT imes • (|∆E + S | + |∆E + S | • initN egs)) evaluations, where |∆E + S | can be factored out. Since the different pre-initialization modes do not employ gradient descent, the number of gradient steps for each entity and relation only depends on the KG and the parameters for the initialization. Theorem 2 The initialization of new entities and relations requires at most O(initT imes • (|∆V + | + |∆R + |)) gradient steps. Proof. As described in the proof of theorem 1 there are at most initT imes • (|∆V + | + |∆R + |) epochs for the whole initialization. Each epoch includes one gradient step for only one entity or relation representation, as the other embeddings are fixed.

figure.head: Theorem 3 AAlgorithm 4
figure.figDesc: change specific epoch requires at most O(|∆E + S | + |∆E - S |) evaluations of the scoring function. Proof. The scoring function is evaluated for all triples insight the T batch (as part of a tuple), which are exactly 2 • (|∆E + S | + |∆E - S |), since we sample only one corrected or corrupted triple for each deleted or added one, respectively. Change Specific Epoch

figure.head: Theorem 4 A
figure.figDesc: change specific epoch requires at most O(|V (t+1) |+|R (t+1) |) gradient steps. Proof. A change specific epoch includes only one backpropagation in the end.

figure.head: Theorem 5 Theorem 6
figure.figDesc: The online method requires at most O(initT imes• |∆E + S | • (1 + initN egs) + csN um • (|∆E + S | + |∆E - S |) + geN um • |S (t+1) |)evaluations of the scoring function. Proof. We already proved the intermediate results for the initialization and the change specific epochs. There are csN um change specific and geN um general epochs in our online method. Each general epoch runs through the entire training set of the current time step t+1 and evaluates the scoring function for each triple (and a corrupted one). The online method requires at most O(initT imes•(|∆V + |+|∆R + |)+ (|V (t+1) | + |R (t+1) |) • (csN um + geN um • num batch)) gradient steps.

Therefore, the scoring function of TransE is defined as

row.cell: TransE As the most representative translational distance model, TransE [2]
learns one vector representation for each entity and one for each relationship.

figure.figDesc: [x] i denotes the i-th entry of the vector x. The intuition is that the components of the relation embedding r describe, whether the corresponding entries in the entity embeddings h and t should match or not. If [r] i is positive, [h] i and [t] i should be quite similar such that the product of the entities' components and therefore the product of all three components is positive, whereas a negative entry in the relation representation constrains the entities to have different values (i.

note.p: e. different signs) for that latent component. However, since h diag(r)t = t diag(r)h ∀h, r, t ∈ R d , DistMult models all relations as symmetric ones, which is an obvious drawback in the case of KGs. DistMult is trained by optimizing the logistic loss.

note.p: In this work, we use terminology commonly used in link prediction work.

The formalism is adjusted to alg. 1. If the offline method is used to recalculate the embeddings for the snapshots of an dynamic KG, the training set and sets of entities and relations have to be marked with the time step.
We make a choice for a reasonable size in ?? in the main paper, but in general, this is up the user or the particular use case.
We only focus on the stability for the actual entity and relation representations, not on the norm vectors of hyperplanes for TransH or the projection vectors for TransD.
